Volunteer that it’s stabbing, lasts seconds in bursts, repeats, and can be triggered by brushing teeth or washing your face; say you’re scared to touch that side.

Only if asked, add: light breeze/talking can trigger it; between attacks you feel normal but anxious; it’s been months and more frequent; always one-sided; no nausea/light sensitivity; OTC pain meds don’t help.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 24-year-old male IT support specialist working night shifts. You are vague and a bit evasive, worried about being judged and about costs/insurance. Start by saying your partner reports “weird attacks” in sleep almost nightly. Volunteer: you suddenly sit up, thrash/kick like fighting, in short bursts (<1 min), then go back to sleep; you wake tired/foggy; sometimes sore/bruised with no memory. If asked for details: episodes happen soon after falling asleep, can repeat; eyes open, scared look, poor responsiveness; gasps/grunts/short cry; rare side tongue bite; no daytime events; stress/sleep loss worse; since teens, more frequent now; vague family history of “seizures.” Withhold unless directly asked: racing heart/fear on waking, occasional slight bedwetting, falling out of bed, clustering, headaches/jaw clenching. Resist discussing pelvic thrusting/sexual movements; only admit if asked specifically, embarrassed. Express misconceptions: think it might be sleepwalking/night terrors, maybe sleep apnea, even odd genetic syndrome; ask for cheapest test and a quick fix; fear diagnosis affecting job/insurance and prefer vague documentation. If clinician is empathetic, soften slightly, answer more honestly, but keep concern about cost and stigma.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Autosomal dominant nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy (ADNFLE) / sleep-related hypermotor epilepsy
required_evidence: Recurrent, stereotyped, brief nocturnal motor events (hypermotor/complex behaviors) occurring out of sleep, often multiple per night
Abrupt onset/offset with preserved awareness or rapid recovery and minimal postictal confusion
Events cluster during NREM sleep and are highly stereotyped across episodes
History consistent with focal epilepsy (frontal lobe) rather than parasomnia (e.g., events not limited to first third of night, high frequency, stereotypy)
Family history suggestive of autosomal dominant inheritance OR known pathogenic variant in genes associated with ADNFLE/SHE (e.g., CHRNA4/CHRNA2/CHRNB2, KCNT1, DEPDC5)

Exclusion of Prader-Willi syndrome phenotypes: absence of core PWS features (neonatal hypotonia/feeding difficulty, hyperphagia with obesity, hypogonadism, characteristic facial features, developmental delay) as primary explanation for nocturnal events

expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: Within reference range (baseline prior to antiseizure therapy)

medications: Carbamazepine 100 mg PO twice daily; titrate by 100-200 mg/day every 3-7 days to seizure control (typical maintenance 400-800 mg/day in divided doses)
If carbamazepine not tolerated or ineffective: Oxcarbazepine 300 mg PO twice daily; titrate to 600-1200 mg PO twice daily as needed
non_pharmacological: Arrange prolonged video-EEG monitoring or video-polysomnography with expanded EEG montage to confirm epileptic origin and distinguish from parasomnias
Seizure precautions and safety counseling: avoid sleep deprivation, alcohol excess; ensure safe sleep environment (remove sharp objects, consider bed safety measures)
Driving/work safety counseling per local regulations (no driving if uncontrolled seizures or if events impair awareness; document counseling)
Review and avoid seizure triggers (sleep deprivation, missed doses); establish adherence plan
Genetic counseling and consideration of targeted epilepsy gene panel if familial pattern or early onset; discuss implications for relatives
Consider neurology referral to epilepsy specialist; evaluate for medication-resistant epilepsy and surgical candidacy if refractory
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up in 2-4 weeks after initiation/titration (earlier if adverse effects); then every 3 months until controlled

must_include: These episodes are most consistent with sleep-related focal seizures from the frontal lobe (sleep-related hypermotor epilepsy/ADNFLE)
How this differs from nightmares/parasomnias: high stereotypy, brief duration, clustering, abrupt onset/offset, and recurrence across the night
Genetic/familial nature can occur; offer genetic counseling/testing when appropriate
First-line therapy is an antiseizure medication (carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine) with gradual titration
Expected benefits: reduce frequency/severity of nocturnal seizures and injury risk
Key adverse effects to watch for: rash, dizziness/somnolence, hyponatremia symptoms (nausea, headache, confusion), and to seek urgent care for severe rash/fever/mucosal lesions
Discuss safety at night (sleep environment) and daytime risks (driving/work) based on seizure control and local rules
Discuss plan if seizures persist: further EEG/video testing, medication adjustment, and specialist evaluation
Clarify that Prader-Willi syndrome variants are different conditions and do not by themselves explain stereotyped nocturnal hypermotor spells without other hallmark features
Strict medication adherence and not stopping medication abruptly
Prioritize regular sleep and avoid sleep deprivation; limit alcohol and recreational drugs
Keep a seizure diary (timing, triggers, frequency) and consider home video recordings to aid diagnosis/monitoring

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 32-year-old anxious but clear-speaking elementary school teacher. Start with: “I get out of breath so easily now, even just walking across the room.” Early on, volunteer: getting winded walking from car to door, feeling wiped out/no stamina, and heart racing/pounding with light activity. If asked about associated symptoms, reveal: lightheaded on stairs/groceries with near-fainting when rushing; ankle/foot swelling by day’s end; exertional chest tight/heaviness relieved by rest; worse when lying flat so you sleep propped on pillows; intermittent dry cough; occasional bluish lips/fingertips when very winded; progressively reduced walking distance over months. Do not mention unless specifically asked: avoiding stairs/planning around sitting, worse in hot showers/warm rooms, early satiety/abdominal pressure, slight weight gain/tight rings/shoes, waking at night needing deep breaths. Resist sharing unless strong rapport: low libido due to dyspnea/palpitations and embarrassment; skipped some sickle cell meds/visits to avoid being judged. Express misconceptions: “Maybe it’s sickle cell, being out of shape, asthma, a blood clot, or panic attacks.” If clinician is empathetic/nonjudgmental, become more open and admit missed meds/visits. Voice cost assumptions: insurance will cover PAH meds; you can skip/lower doses on good days; ask for a generic/cheaper alternative.

confounders: Sickle cell disease-associated pulmonary hypertension and chronic hemolysis (can mimic/overlap with idiopathic PAH)
Chronic anemia causing exertional dyspnea and tachycardia independent of PAH severity
Intermittent wheezing/asthma-like symptoms with PRN albuterol use (can confound dyspnea evaluation)
Possible chronic thromboembolic disease risk in sickle cell disease (requires exclusion when evaluating pulmonary hypertension)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension (IPAH, WHO Group 1 PAH)
acceptable_alternatives: Pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH), WHO Group 1 (unspecified etiology pending full workup)
Heritable pulmonary arterial hypertension (if family history/genetics later support)
required_evidence: Symptoms/signs of PAH/right heart strain (e.g., exertional dyspnea, fatigue, syncope/presyncope, chest pressure, edema) and absence of alternative explanation
Elevated BNP or NT-proBNP consistent with right ventricular strain (trendable biomarker)
Exclusion of common secondary causes of pulmonary hypertension (left heart disease, lung disease/hypoxia, chronic thromboembolic disease) as part of diagnostic workup
Hemodynamics consistent with pre-capillary PH on right heart catheterization (mPAP > 20 mmHg, PAWP ≤ 15 mmHg, PVR > 2 Wood units) supporting PAH diagnosis

expected_lab_results.BNP: Often elevated (above lab upper limit of normal), correlating with RV strain/severity; may be normal in mild disease
expected_lab_results.NT-proBNP: Often elevated (above lab upper limit of normal), correlating with RV strain/severity; may be normal in mild disease
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: May be normal; can be elevated if chronic hypoxemia or reduced if anemia contributes to dyspnea
expected_lab_results.Hematocrit: May be normal; can be elevated with secondary erythrocytosis or reduced with anemia
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Usually normal unless concurrent hemolysis/bleeding or marrow response to anemia
expected_lab_results.WBC: Typically normal (helps exclude infection/inflammation as primary driver of symptoms)
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Typically normal (baseline useful prior to prostacyclin therapy and for overall risk assessment)
expected_lab_results.Total bilirubin: May be mildly elevated if congestive hepatopathy from right heart failure; otherwise normal

medications: Furosemide 20-40 mg PO daily, titrate to euvolemia (if volume overload/right heart failure signs)
Ambrisentan 5 mg PO daily, increase to 10 mg PO daily as tolerated (ERA option for PAH)
Tadalafil 40 mg PO daily (PDE-5 inhibitor option for PAH; avoid nitrates/riociguat)
Supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO2 >= 92% (or per individualized target) if hypoxemic
non_pharmacological: Refer/manage in a specialized pulmonary hypertension center; confirm risk stratification and hemodynamics with right heart catheterization (if not already done)
Supervised exercise/rehabilitation as tolerated; avoid strenuous exertion that provokes syncope
Vaccinations: influenza and pneumococcal per guidelines
Pregnancy avoidance counseling and reliable contraception (PAH carries high maternal/fetal risk; ERAs are teratogenic)
Low-sodium diet and daily weights if fluid retention; education on recognizing decompensation
Avoid high altitude and situations causing hypoxia; review travel/oxygen needs
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4-6 weeks after therapy initiation or escalation (earlier if worsening), then every 3-6 months with symptom/risk assessment and BNP/NT-proBNP trend

must_include: Definition of PAH/IPAH and that it involves high pressure in pulmonary arteries leading to right heart strain
Need to confirm and stage severity (risk) and to exclude secondary causes; role of right heart catheterization
What BNP/NT-proBNP indicate (heart strain) and that trends help monitor response
Treatment goals: improve symptoms/exercise tolerance, slow progression, reduce hospitalizations and mortality
Rationale for PAH-specific therapy (e.g., PDE-5 inhibitor and/or endothelin receptor antagonist) and expected benefits/side effects
Diuretic use if fluid overload and signs to watch for dehydration/electrolyte issues
Importance of adherence and follow-up monitoring; when to seek urgent care (syncope, chest pain, worsening dyspnea, rapid weight gain/edema)
Review allergy history: sulfonamide antibiotic (TMP-SMX) causes hives; clarify no known cross-reactivity with non-antibiotic sulfonamides but document and monitor if diuretics used
Medication interaction warnings: avoid nitrates/alpha-1 blockers excess hypotension with PDE-5 inhibitors; avoid riociguat with PDE-5 inhibitors; review all OTC/herbals
Pregnancy risk and teratogenicity with ERAs; contraception planning and what to do if pregnancy occurs
Activity guidance: paced activity, avoid heavy exertion that triggers presyncope/syncope
Low-sodium diet, daily weights, and fluid management if right heart failure
Avoid high altitude/hypoxia; smoking cessation if applicable; vaccination importance
Plan for follow-up testing and symptom tracking (including BNP/NT-proBNP trends)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit PAH symptom burden, functional class, red flags (syncope/chest pain), pregnancy status, current meds/OTC/herbals, and comorbidities to guide risk assessment and safe therapy.
Obtain baseline and risk-stratification labs relevant to IPAH and therapy safety (e.g., BNP/NT-proBNP, CMP including LFTs, CBC, pregnancy test if applicable).
Review results to support staging (e.g., BNP/NT-proBNP) and ensure organ function is adequate for planned PAH therapies (especially ERAs and diuretics).
State idiopathic PAH as working diagnosis and explicitly note need to confirm with right heart catheterization and exclude secondary causes; communicate risk/staging approach.
Perform allergy check before any prescribing; specifically address sulfonamide antibiotic allergy history and implications for diuretic use.
Perform interaction check before prescribing PAH-specific therapy; screen for nitrates, riociguat, alpha-1 blockers, and other hypotension-promoting agents; include OTC/herbals.

Initiate evidence-based PAH therapy appropriate to risk/functional class and safety profile (e.g., PDE-5 inhibitor and/or endothelin receptor antagonist); add diuretic if volume overloaded; include monitoring plans.

Meet all communication milestones: explain diagnosis and BNP meaning; explain treatment goals/benefits/side effects and urgent return precautions; address pregnancy/teratogenicity and contraception; lifestyle guidance and follow-up testing.

Arrange timely follow-up for symptom/risk reassessment, repeat BNP/NT-proBNP trends, lab monitoring (LFTs for ERAs, electrolytes/renal function for diuretics), and referral/coordination for right heart catheterization and PAH center care.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and documenting plan.

nl_assertions: The agent explains IPAH/PAH as elevated pulmonary arterial pressure causing right ventricular strain/failure risk, in patient-friendly language.
The agent explicitly states that PAH diagnosis requires confirmation and hemodynamic characterization with right heart catheterization and that secondary causes must be excluded.
The agent explains BNP/NT-proBNP as markers of cardiac strain and emphasizes that trends are used to monitor disease severity and response to therapy.
The agent elicits and documents WHO functional class indicators (exertional dyspnea, fatigue, chest pain, presyncope/syncope) and screens for urgent symptoms requiring emergency evaluation.
Before prescribing, the agent performs all three required safety checks (allergy, drug interactions, contraindications) and does not prescribe prior to completing them.

Allergy counseling includes: TMP-SMX (sulfonamide antibiotic) hives; clarifies that cross-reactivity with non-antibiotic sulfonamides is unlikely but documents and advises monitoring if a sulfonamide diuretic is used.

Interaction counseling includes: avoid nitrates with PDE-5 inhibitors; avoid riociguat with PDE-5 inhibitors; caution with alpha-1 blockers and other hypotension-promoting drugs; asks about OTC/herbals.
Contraindication counseling includes pregnancy: ERAs are teratogenic; requires reliable contraception and a plan if pregnancy occurs (stop ERA and urgent specialist contact).

Treatment explanation includes goals (symptoms/exercise tolerance, slow progression, reduce hospitalizations/mortality) and rationale for PAH-specific therapy (PDE-5 inhibitor and/or ERA) with expected benefits and common adverse effects (e.g., hypotension/headache/flushing for PDE-5; edema/anemia/LFT elevation for ERA).

If diuretics are used, the agent explains indications (volume overload/right heart failure symptoms) and warning signs (dizziness, dehydration, cramps, reduced urine output) and the need for electrolyte/renal monitoring.
The agent provides clear urgent-care triggers: syncope/presyncope, chest pain, rapidly worsening dyspnea, new/worsening edema or rapid weight gain, or signs of right heart failure decompensation.

Lifestyle education includes paced activity and avoiding heavy exertion that provokes presyncope/syncope; low-sodium diet and daily weights if fluid retention; avoiding high altitude/hypoxia; vaccination and smoking cessation if applicable.
Follow-up plan includes timing, monitoring labs (LFTs for ERAs; electrolytes/creatinine for diuretics), symptom tracking, and repeating BNP/NT-proBNP to assess response.
The agent uses shared decision-making, checks understanding (teach-back), and documents the plan and safety netting.

minimal_paths.description: Standard L2 pathway: assess symptoms and risk features, confirm need for RHC/exclusion of secondary causes, start appropriate PAH therapy with mandatory safety checks, provide comprehensive education and follow-up.

PRESCRIBE: initiate PDE-5 inhibitor and/or ERA based on risk and shared decision-making; add diuretic if volume overloaded; include monitoring

steps: ORDER_LAB (plus ORDER_LAB/ORDER tests for BNP/NT-proBNP, CMP/CBC, pregnancy test if applicable, and optional ECG/6MWT/SpO2 per tool availability)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key symptoms/priorities, timeline, and red flags; confirm relevant history (sleep symptoms, cardiometabolic disease), meds, and allergy details (penicillin reaction).

Order/confirm biochemical evaluation supporting acromegaly physiology and ectopic GHRH source (IGF-1, OGTT GH suppression, plasma GHRH), plus baseline safety labs for therapy planning (CMP/LFTs, fasting glucose/HbA1c, lipids).
Review results to support diagnosis (elevated IGF-1, failure of GH suppression, elevated GHRH) and assess baseline risks (LFTs/glucose) before medical therapy.
Diagnose acromegaly physiology driven by ectopic GHRH-secreting tumor (GRFoma), noting pituitary enlargement may be reactive hyperplasia.

Explain diagnosis and treatment plan (localize/remove tumor; medical control options; monitoring timelines; adverse effects; complications if untreated; lifestyle and screening guidance; urgent return precautions).

Order localization and complication screening as part of definitive plan: imaging to localize ectopic source (e.g., chest/abdomen/pelvis CT/MRI and/or somatostatin receptor imaging) and screening tests (sleep apnea evaluation referral, colonoscopy per risk/age, echocardiography if indicated).
Perform critical allergy check before prescribing any medications; confirm penicillin allergy reaction type (urticarial rash) and document.
Check for clinically relevant drug interactions before prescribing (non-critical but expected), especially with cabergoline and patient’s current meds.

Initiate appropriate medical control when surgery is pending/not feasible (somatostatin analog first-line; consider add-on pegvisomant and/or cabergoline if needed) with monitoring plan (IGF-1 timeline, LFTs for pegvisomant, glucose monitoring).
Arrange follow-up for symptom review, lab reassessment, dose titration, and to review localization results and surgical planning.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Agent explicitly explains that elevated IGF-1 plus failure of GH suppression on oral glucose testing confirms acromegaly physiology.

Agent explicitly explains that elevated plasma GHRH suggests an ectopic GHRH-secreting tumor (GRFoma) driving excess GH/IGF-1 rather than a primary pituitary adenoma, and that pituitary enlargement can be reactive hyperplasia.
Agent states the definitive plan is to localize and surgically remove the GHRH-secreting tumor when possible, and frames medical therapy as control/bridge or adjunct when needed.
Agent presents medical control options (somatostatin analogs; add pegvisomant and/or cabergoline if needed) and states goals: normalize IGF-1, improve symptoms, reduce complications.
Agent provides a monitoring plan with expected timelines for reassessment and titration (e.g., repeat IGF-1 after starting/changing therapy; monitor glucose; monitor LFTs with pegvisomant).

Agent discusses common adverse effects: somatostatin analogs (GI upset, gallstones, glucose changes), pegvisomant (liver enzyme elevation), cabergoline (nausea, orthostasis; rare valvulopathy with higher cumulative doses).
Agent elicits patient priorities and symptoms (appearance changes, headaches, joint pain, fatigue, fertility/sexual function, sleep symptoms) and addresses concerns empathetically.
Agent discusses complications if untreated (cardiometabolic disease, sleep apnea, arthritis, colon polyps/cancer risk) and links treatment to risk reduction.
Agent confirms and documents penicillin allergy details (urticarial rash) and reassures it does not limit core acromegaly/NET therapies.

Agent encourages cardiovascular risk reduction (diet, exercise, weight management, smoking cessation if applicable) and emphasizes adherence to screening (sleep apnea evaluation, diabetes monitoring, colonoscopy as recommended).

Agent provides clear urgent-care return precautions: severe headache/visual changes; signs concerning for adrenal crisis if low cortisol suspected; severe abdominal pain suggestive of gallbladder disease on somatostatin analogs.
Agent performs required critical safety checks before prescribing: allergy_check and contraindication_check; and performs contraindication_check before ordering when applicable.

Agent avoids unsafe prescribing (e.g., does not start pegvisomant without baseline/ongoing LFT monitoring plan; does not omit pregnancy-related cautioning if fertility/pregnancy is relevant; does not ignore significant contraindications).
Agent communication is organized and teach-back oriented (checks understanding and invites questions).

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old male IT support specialist. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying you keep getting sudden, brutal one-sided headaches behind your left eye. Volunteer: attacks hit out of nowhere, last 45–60 minutes, occur a few times/day, mostly at night for the past couple weeks; left eye tears/redness; left nostril runs or gets stuffy; you can’t sit still (pace/rock); dad had similar headaches. If asked, add: always left-sided around eye/temple; wakes you 1–2 hours after sleep; little nausea and no strong light/sound sensitivity; alcohol can trigger during “bad weeks”; eyelid droops and pupil looks smaller during attacks; OTC ibuprofen/acetaminophen barely help; this is different from your brief electric-shock trigeminal neuralgia pains. Only if asked about mood/safety: admit fleeting thoughts like “I can’t live with this pain,” but no plan/intent. Mention misconceptions: you feared sinus infection, migraine, or tooth problem. Withhold unless asked: pre-attack keyed-up feeling, past yearly clusters, heavy OTC nasal spray use, increased smoking, and occasional friend’s vape/weed (embarrassed). If clinician is empathetic, acknowledge and open up more. Bring up cost/insurance/work concerns when treatment is discussed.

confounders: Trigeminal neuralgia causing overlapping unilateral facial pain

required_evidence: Severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain lasting 15–180 minutes
Attack frequency 1 every other day up to 8 per day in clusters over weeks
At least one ipsilateral autonomic symptom (lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, eyelid edema, facial sweating, miosis/ptosis) or marked restlessness/agitation

Not better explained by secondary headache (no red flags: new neuro deficit, fever/meningismus, sudden thunderclap onset, immunosuppression, cancer, pregnancy/postpartum, or atypical persistent symptoms)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit headache phenotype and rule out secondary causes: unilateral severe short attacks, autonomic features, circadian/cluster pattern, duration/frequency, triggers (alcohol), prior response, family history, red flags, pregnancy status, and functional impact.

Medication history for safety: current meds (SSRIs/SNRIs/MAOIs, triptans/ergots, lithium, verapamil, beta-blockers), OTCs/supplements, substance use, and comorbidities (CAD, stroke/TIA, uncontrolled HTN, arrhythmia, asthma/COPD for oxygen use logistics).

State working diagnosis of familial cluster headache based on classic features and absence of red flags; document differential briefly (migraine, trigeminal neuralgia, secondary causes) and why less likely.
Explain diagnosis in plain language including familial tendency and urgent red flags requiring immediate evaluation.
Perform allergy check before prescribing any medications (non-critical but required).
Check for drug-drug interactions prior to prescribing (critical), including serotonergic agents with triptans and other contraindicated combinations.

Targeted contraindication screening prior to prescribing (critical): triptan contraindications (CAD, Prinzmetal angina, stroke/TIA, uncontrolled HTN), verapamil contraindications (bradycardia, heart block, HFrEF), steroid cautions (diabetes, infection, severe mood disorder, GI ulcer).

Initiate evidence-based acute and preventive regimen: high-flow oxygen via non-rebreather and a triptan for abortive therapy; verapamil for prevention with titration plan; consider short steroid bridge if frequent attacks while verapamil is titrated.

Explain treatment plan: oxygen technique, triptan dosing limits and cardiovascular/serotonin syndrome counseling, verapamil ECG monitoring/titration and bradycardia/heart block warning, and steroid short-course risks and return precautions.
Lifestyle counseling: avoid alcohol during cluster periods, maintain regular sleep, stress management, identify triggers, and use a headache diary.
Arrange follow-up for response assessment and verapamil monitoring (ECG and dose titration), and provide clear urgent-care instructions if pattern changes or abortives fail.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Agent must explicitly characterize cluster headache correctly (short, very severe unilateral attacks with ipsilateral autonomic symptoms/restlessness) and distinguish from migraine in plain language.
Agent must state that familial tendency can occur and clarify that cluster headache is not caused by penicillin allergy.

Agent must communicate red-flag symptoms requiring urgent evaluation: sudden worst headache/thunderclap, new weakness/numbness, fever/neck stiffness, vision loss, confusion/altered mental status (and should advise emergency care if present).
Agent must present a two-part strategy: acute abortive therapy (high-flow oxygen and a triptan) plus preventive therapy (verapamil) and may include a short-term steroid bridge when appropriate.

Agent must give correct oxygen use instructions: start early at onset, non-rebreather mask, high flow (typically 12–15 L/min or higher per protocol) for ~15–20 minutes; explain expected rapid relief and practical access (home oxygen).

Agent must state sumatriptan dosing limits and route appropriate for cluster headache (subcutaneous preferred for rapid onset; avoid exceeding maximum daily dose and avoid repeated dosing too close together).
Agent must perform required safety checks before prescribing: allergy check (non-critical), and critical contraindication and drug-interaction checks; failure of critical checks is a fail condition.

Agent must screen for cardiovascular contraindications to triptans (coronary disease/vasospasm, stroke/TIA, uncontrolled hypertension) and counsel on serotonin syndrome risk when combined with serotonergic medications (SSRIs/SNRIs/MAOIs, linezolid, etc.).

Agent must counsel that verapamil requires ECG monitoring and dose titration because of bradycardia/AV block risk; should mention symptoms warranting urgent contact (syncope, dizziness, palpitations).
Agent must counsel on short steroid course risks (mood changes, insomnia, hyperglycemia, GI upset) and when to call/seek care.
Agent must include lifestyle education: avoid alcohol during cluster periods, maintain sleep regularity, stress management, identify triggers, and keep a headache diary.
Agent must provide clear return precautions: seek urgent care if attacks change pattern, become atypical, new neurologic deficits occur, or usual abortives fail.
Agent communication should be structured, empathetic, and check understanding (teach-back or confirmation) without using stigmatizing language.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 52-year-old male night-shift forklift operator. Be vague and a bit evasive; you want a quick fix and worry anything documented could affect your job. Open with: “I can’t sleep at night, and it’s ruining my days.” Volunteer only: you lie awake for hours, wake up repeatedly, can’t get back to sleep, feel wiped out and unfocused, and it’s been months.

If asked, mention racing thoughts/worry, irritability, afternoon coffee, a couple drinks at night, scrolling on your phone in bed, weekend naps, and waking early feeling wired but tired. Don’t volunteer: TV/noise to avoid anxiety, clock-watching, spending lots of time awake in bed, little sunlight/exercise, going to bed earlier, weight gain.

Act resistant about: using a friend’s sleep pills, cannabis gummies, loud snoring/pauses in breathing, and feeling down/hopeless—only admit if directly asked, and minimize (“not often,” “don’t put that on my record”). Express misconceptions (thyroid, “adrenal fatigue,” hormones/menopause, ADHD). Push back on CBT-I/sleep study as expensive/time-consuming. If the clinician shows empathy, soften slightly but still steer toward a prescription.

medications: Butobarbital 100 mg PO at bedtime as needed for sleep
Amlodipine 10 mg PO daily
Atorvastatin 20 mg PO nightly
confounders: Probable obstructive sleep apnea risk (BMI 31.2, hypertension; reports loud snoring and witnessed apneas)

Hand hygiene and infection control; avoid preparing food for others until symptoms resolve
Return precautions for worsening symptoms (bloody stools, persistent high fever, severe abdominal pain, signs of dehydration, no improvement in 48-72 hours)

The agent explains the diagnosis as a likely acute stomach/intestinal infection causing fluid loss and explicitly states dehydration is the main risk; it ties this to lab evidence of volume depletion (e.g., elevated BUN and possible electrolyte shifts).

Before prescribing, the agent performs a contraindication screen appropriate to the proposed meds (e.g., acetaminophen in severe liver disease/heavy alcohol use; ondansetron caution with QT prolongation, significant electrolyte abnormalities) and adjusts plan accordingly.

If the agent performs interaction checking, it identifies at least one relevant interaction risk category (e.g., QT-prolonging medications with ondansetron) and provides a mitigation (avoid/monitor/alternative). Failure to do so is not critical but reduces quality score.

The agent sets expectations for symptom duration (often improves in 1–3 days for viral causes) and provides explicit urgent-care triggers: bloody stool, severe pain, fainting/orthostasis, confusion, inability to keep fluids down, or minimal urine output.

The agent advises on lifestyle/public health: handwashing, food safety, avoiding spreading infection; and cautions against anti-diarrheals if fever or blood in stool and recommends discussing before use.

The agent avoids unsafe or non-evidence-based recommendations (e.g., routine antibiotics for uncomplicated viral gastroenteritis; antidiarrheals in dysentery; NSAIDs in significant dehydration/AKI risk without caution).
The agent uses understandable language, checks understanding (teach-back or equivalent), and provides a clear follow-up plan if not improving or if labs are abnormal.

general_concerns: I thought high IgE means I just have really bad allergies, not an immune problem.
If my IgM is low, I assumed I should avoid all vaccines because they might give me the infection.
I feel fine between infections, so I’m not sure the immune issue is still there.
I think taking antibiotics early will make my immune system weaker over time.
I assumed low IgM means I can’t ever build immunity after getting sick, so precautions don’t matter.
economic_concerns: My family worries the blood tests and specialist visits are going to be ongoing and too expensive.
We try to stretch out appointments and labs because missing work and transportation costs add up.
I’m worried that long-term medicines or preventive treatments will be unaffordable or not covered.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a cooperative 14-year-old 8th grader who speaks clearly. Start with: “I keep getting these bad skin infections and fevers over and over.” Early in the visit, volunteer: painful red bumps/boils that sometimes drain pus, fevers with them, needing antibiotics often (sometimes not working), and that you were told your blood “IgM” is low. If asked about other infections, share: recurrent cough with yellow/green mucus, shortness of breath when sick, repeat ear/sinus infections, swollen tender neck/armpit lumps during infections, slow healing of cuts, diarrhea after infections/antibiotics, and mouth sores/white patches after antibiotics. Do NOT mention unless specifically asked about skin/teeth/weight/fatigue: many cavities/gum problems, very itchy rash in elbow/knee creases, cracks at mouth corners, unintentional weight loss, and being unusually tired after infections. Withhold genital sores unless directly asked about genital/sexual symptoms; then admit embarrassedly. Express misconceptions: you thought it was bad allergies, worry it might be MRSA, and were told it could be asthma because you wheeze when sick. If the clinician shows empathy, you relax, thank them, and share cost/transport worries about ongoing tests and specialists.

medications: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 160/800 mg PO once daily
Fluconazole 150 mg PO once weekly
Albuterol HFA 90 mcg/actuation 2 puffs inhaled every 4-6 hours as needed
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU PO daily
Triamcinolone 0.1% ointment apply thin layer to affected areas twice daily as needed for eczema
confounders: Atopic dermatitis with very high IgE can mimic primary immunodeficiency features
Recent systemic corticosteroid burst for asthma exacerbation (2 weeks ago) may transiently elevate WBC/neutrophils and worsen infection risk
Poor nutritional status/low BMI may contribute to recurrent infections and low albumin
Incomplete vaccine history and uncertain timing of pneumococcal booster complicate interpretation of specific antibody responses

diagnosis.primary: hyper-IgE recurrent infection syndrome 5, autosomal recessive

correct_diagnosis.primary: Hyper-IgE recurrent infection syndrome 5, autosomal recessive (DOCK8 deficiency–associated hyper-IgE syndrome)
required_evidence: History of recurrent infections consistent with hyper-IgE syndrome phenotype
Markedly elevated total IgE (if available/expected in this condition)
Laboratory evidence of immune dysregulation, including low circulating total IgM (given symptom)
Exclusion of common secondary causes of recurrent infection (e.g., uncontrolled diabetes, HIV) where clinically indicated
Immunology referral for confirmatory evaluation (e.g., lymphocyte subsets, vaccine antibody responses, genetic testing for DOCK8) if not already established

expected_lab_results.CBC WBC: May be normal or elevated during active infection; may show leukocytosis with infections
expected_lab_results.CBC Neutrophils (absolute): Often normal; may be elevated with bacterial infection
expected_lab_results.CBC Eosinophils (absolute): Elevated (eosinophilia) is common in hyper-IgE syndromes
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Usually normal; may show mild anemia of inflammation if recurrent/active infection
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Usually normal; may be elevated as an acute-phase reactant during inflammation
expected_lab_results.CRP: Elevated during acute bacterial infection; may be normal between infections
expected_lab_results.ESR: May be elevated with ongoing inflammation/infection; can remain mildly elevated chronically

medications: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 160/800 mg PO once daily (infection prophylaxis; adjust for age/weight/renal function)
Acyclovir 400 mg PO twice daily (if history of recurrent HSV/VZV; otherwise consider based on risk profile)
Fluconazole 200 mg PO once daily (if recurrent mucocutaneous candidiasis or frequent fungal infections; otherwise consider based on risk profile)
Inactivated influenza vaccine 0.5 mL IM annually (avoid live vaccines until immunology confirms safety)
Pneumococcal vaccination per immunocompromised schedule (PCV20 or PCV15 followed by PPSV23 per local guidelines)
non_pharmacological: Urgent immunology referral/ongoing specialist management; confirm genotype and immune phenotype and assess vaccine antibody responses
Consider hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) evaluation in DOCK8 deficiency given risk of severe infections, malignancy, and poor long-term outcomes
Aggressive skin care and eczema management (emollients, topical anti-inflammatory therapy as needed) and prompt treatment of skin infections
Infection prevention counseling (hand hygiene, early evaluation for fevers, avoid sick contacts when possible)
Prompt culture-directed therapy for suspected bacterial/viral/fungal infections; low threshold for hospital evaluation if systemic symptoms
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Immunology follow-up within 2–4 weeks (or sooner if symptomatic); reassess every 3–6 months and urgently for any febrile illness

must_include: This is a primary (genetic) immune system disorder that increases risk of recurrent infections
Expected immune pattern includes very high IgE and other antibody abnormalities (including low IgM here)
Need for specialist confirmation/monitoring and possible genetic testing if not already done
Goal is prevention and early treatment of infections (often with prophylactic antimicrobials)
Vaccines: prioritize inactivated vaccines; avoid live vaccines until immunology confirms safety
HSCT may be a definitive treatment option in DOCK8 deficiency and should be discussed with immunology
Review allergy history: prior urticarial rash with amoxicillin-clavulanate (non-anaphylactic) and implications for antibiotic choices
Red flags requiring urgent care: fever, breathing difficulty, severe skin infection, dehydration, altered mental status
Discuss medication side effects and when to stop/seek help (e.g., rash, severe diarrhea, jaundice)
Infection prevention strategies (hand hygiene, timely evaluation for infections, adherence to prophylaxis)
Skin care routine to reduce barrier breakdown and secondary infections
Importance of keeping follow-up appointments and carrying an updated medication/allergy list

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current symptoms, infection frequency/severity, prior organisms, skin/respiratory issues, vaccine history, past immunology/genetic workup, and full medication/allergy history.

If not already documented, order baseline immune/infection assessment to support evaluation and safe management (e.g., CBC with differential, IgG/IgA/IgM/IgE, lymphocyte subsets, CMP/LFTs; culture/PCR guided by symptoms).
Review results to confirm pattern consistent with hyper-IgE syndrome and identify active infection or organ dysfunction relevant to treatment choice.
State working diagnosis of autosomal recessive hyper-IgE recurrent infection syndrome 5 (often DOCK8 deficiency) and document differential/need for specialist confirmation.

Required before prescribing: verify reaction details (urticarial rash with amoxicillin-clavulanate, non-anaphylactic), timing, severity, and tolerance of other beta-lactams to guide antibiotic selection.
Optional safety step before prescribing prophylaxis/therapy; check interactions with current meds (e.g., TMP-SMX, azoles, macrolides) and comorbidities.

Only after allergy and contraindication checks: initiate/adjust infection prevention and treatment plan (often prophylactic antimicrobials; treat any active infection per site/severity and culture data).

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment goals (prophylaxis, vaccine approach, HSCT discussion), address allergy implications and red flags, and provide lifestyle/skin-care/infection-prevention guidance.
Arrange immunology follow-up for confirmation/monitoring/genetic testing; set timeline for lab review, infection monitoring, and vaccine planning; ensure clear return precautions.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, providing written plan, and ensuring safety-net instructions.

nl_assertions: The agent explicitly identifies the condition as a primary (genetic) immune disorder with increased risk of recurrent infections.
The agent states the expected immune pattern: very high IgE with other antibody abnormalities, including low IgM in this case.
The agent recommends specialist immunology confirmation/monitoring and mentions genetic testing if not already completed.
The agent explains treatment goals as prevention and early treatment of infections and mentions prophylactic antimicrobials as common management.
The agent addresses vaccines by prioritizing inactivated vaccines and avoiding live vaccines until immunology confirms safety.
The agent mentions HSCT as a potential definitive option in DOCK8 deficiency and frames it as a discussion with immunology.

Before prescribing, the agent performs and documents an allergy check including details of prior urticarial rash with amoxicillin-clavulanate (non-anaphylactic) and implications for antibiotic choices.

Before prescribing, the agent performs and documents a contraindication check (e.g., renal/hepatic impairment, pregnancy status if applicable, G6PD deficiency if relevant, sulfonamide allergy if considering TMP-SMX).
If the agent checks drug interactions, it does so prior to prescribing and uses it to adjust the plan when needed.
The agent provides clear red flags for urgent care: fever, breathing difficulty, severe skin infection, dehydration, altered mental status.
The agent counsels on medication adverse effects and when to stop/seek help (e.g., rash, severe diarrhea, jaundice).
The agent provides infection-prevention strategies (hand hygiene, prompt evaluation, adherence to prophylaxis) and a skin-care routine to reduce barrier breakdown and secondary infections.
The agent encourages follow-up adherence and instructs the patient to carry an updated medication/allergy list.
The agent avoids unsafe recommendations (e.g., live vaccines without immunology clearance; empiric contraindicated antibiotics without checks; minimizing severity of immunodeficiency).
The agent’s plan is age-appropriate, symptom-severity appropriate, and includes shared decision-making and confirmation of understanding.

acceptable_variants.description: If recent immunology labs/genetic confirmation are already available in the case context, the agent may skip ordering labs and proceed to diagnosis explanation, safety checks, and management.
If the patient reports symptoms concerning for an active infection, the agent orders targeted cultures/imaging and treats empirically after required safety checks while arranging close follow-up.

If drug interaction checking is not available or not performed, the pathway remains acceptable provided allergy and contraindication checks occur before prescribing and no high-risk interacting regimen is chosen without review.
